content:
6 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Last Tuesday I had two Chrome DevTools tabs open side by side, network panel pulled up in both. Same landing page, rebuilt twice: once in Next.js 16 with the App Router, once in Astro 5. The Next.js version shipped 214KB of JS before a single button worked. The Astro version shipped 6KB. I sat there for a solid minute just staring at the gap, thinking some version of “we’re an idiot for using this for a blog.” He wasn’t wrong, exactly, but he also wasn’t entirely right, and that gap between “wasn’t wrong” and “wasn’t right” is basically the whole conversation people are having about these two frameworks right now. The actual difference, not the marketing copy Next.js assumes interactivity is the default state of a page. Every component might need to hydrate, so the runtime ships along for the ride even on pages that are 95% static text. Astro assumes the opposite: a page is static until you explicitly tell it otherwise, island by island. --- // src/pages/index.astro import Counter from "../components/Counter.tsx"; ---

Welcome

This entire page is static HTML. Zero JS shipped for it.

That client:visible directive is the whole trick. Nothing hydrates until that component is in the viewport, and nothing outside of it ships JavaScript at all. Compare that to a Next.js page where the entire tree, even the parts that are just formatted text, rides along with the router and hydration machinery because that's the deal you signed up for when you chose the framework. Neither approach is a mistake. They’re built for different bets about what your page actually needs to do. When the Next.js tax is worth paying If your app is genuinely an application, meaning state persists across navigations, users are logged in, there’s a cart or a dashboard or real-time data, the “cost” of shipping React everywhere stops being a cost and starts being the thing you’re building. Trying to force that into Astro’s island model means you end up recreating a client router and a state management layer by hand, badly, which is exactly the trap a lot of “just use Astro for everything” takes fall into. // app/dashboard/page.tsx export default async function DashboardPage() { const data = await getServerData(); return ( ); }LiveMetrics here is a client component doing polling, websockets, whatever. In an app like this, most of the page is interactive most of the time. The App Router's model, with server components handling the static shell and client components picking up the interactive parts, is doing real work. The 85-250KB of JS Next.js ships isn't waste, it's the application. When it’s just weight The problem is how many teams reach for Next.js by default for things that are, structurally, documents. Marketing pages, blogs, docs sites, marketing-with-a-contact-form sites. These don’t need a client router. They don’t need hydration boundaries. They need HTML, fast, with maybe one or two islands of interactivity (a nav toggle, a newsletter form, a pricing calculator). --- import NewsletterForm from "../components/NewsletterForm.tsx"; --- PricingTable here can be a plain Astro component, zero JS, even if it's rendering complex conditional logic, because none of that logic needs to run in the browser. Only the form needs a hydration budget, and client:idle means it won't even claim that budget until the main thread is free. This is the case that DevTools screenshot was actually making. A marketing site doesn't need to be a single-page app. Five pages of text and one form were never going to lose anything by shipping less JS, and it clearly gained something. The content collections thing people undersell The bundle size stat gets all the attention, but Astro’s content collections API is arguably the bigger quality-of-life win for anything blog-shaped. // content.config.ts import { defineCollection, z } from "astro:content";const posts = defineCollection({ schema: z.object({ title: z.string(), publishDate: z.date(), tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]), }), });export const collections = { posts };Now every markdown file in that collection is type-checked against that schema at build time, and you get autocomplete on frontmatter fields in your editor. Next.js can absolutely do this too, with MDX plus a schema library bolted on, but it’s DIY. Astro baked it in, and if your site is mostly content, that matters more day to day than the bundle size number that gets screenshotted into Slack. The part where Astro also has to grow up Astro’s pitch works great until your “mostly static site” wants one feature that’s genuinely stateful, and this is where the arguments in favor of it get quieter. Say marketing wants a pricing calculator that updates live as someone drags a slider, remembers their choice across a couple of pages, and syncs to a cart. Technically you can do this with islands and some manual state passed through the URL or a tiny shared store, but now you’re hand-building the exact plumbing that a full framework gives you automatically. A full week can disappear into wiring up cross-island communication for a feature that would have been a useState and a context provider in a normal React app. --- // works fine for isolated interactivity import PriceSlider from "../components/PriceSlider.tsx"; import CartBadge from "../components/CartBadge.tsx"; --- Two islands, no shared state by default. If PriceSlider needs to update CartBadge, you're reaching for nanostores or something similar, which works, but it's an extra dependency and an extra mental model your team now has to hold in their heads alongside "when does this hydrate." That's a fine tradeoff for a handful of interactive widgets. It stops being fine somewhere around "our marketing site quietly grew a checkout flow," which happens more often than teams plan for. The migration story is messier than the benchmark The benchmark screenshots (9KB vs 214KB, pick your favorite number) are real, but they compare a finished Astro build against a finished Next.js build. They don’t capture the cost of migrating an existing Next.js marketing site to Astro, which usually means rewriting every page’s data fetching, re-doing your component library’s integration (does your design system have an Astro adapter, or are you wrapping every single component as a React island now), and re-testing SEO metadata that used to live in generateMetadata and now lives somewhere else entirely. For a five-page brochure site, fine, a weekend project. For the sprawling fifty-page marketing site most mid-size companies actually have, it's a quarter of work to save some kilobytes that users on a decent connection barely notice anyway. That last point is worth sitting with. Bundle size matters most on slow connections and low-end devices, which is a real and important audience, but it’s not the only audience, and “we cut JS by 95 percent” sounds more dramatic in a blog post than it often feels in a Lighthouse score once your Next.js app already has good code splitting and streaming in place. Nobody has to pick a side, actually The annoying, boring answer, the one nobody wants because it doesn’t make a good hot take, is that these solve different problems and the “vs” in every blog post title is doing a lot of unearned work. If you’re building something app-shaped, the App Router’s tradeoffs make sense and the JS weight buys you something real. If you’re building something document-shaped, shipping a full client router for a page that has one button is just tax, and Astro was built specifically to not charge it. The marketing site stayed in Astro. The actual product dashboard stayed in Next.js. Nobody had to defend a framework choice in a meeting, because the two apps were never competing for the same job in the first place. Which is a little unsatisfying as a conclusion, I know. There’s no winner here, just two tools that happen to share a demo of “look, we can also render a blog post.” Sources: • Astro vs Next.js 2026: 9KB vs 463KB JS (Tested) • Astro vs Next.js 16: Which to Choose in 2026 • Astro vs Next.js (2026): Real Benchmarks After Building With Both • Next.js vs Astro: Which Framework Should You Choose in 2026? • Next.js vs Astro for content sites and blogs: an honest comparison
